message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="Driver rotation: Luca is Driver for Task #11">
Driver rotation complete. We're now working on Task #11: Implementation Step 1 — Navigation + Dashboard skeleton.

**Luca Palmieri** is the new Driver (the only agent who may modify files).
**Marty Cagan** has been respawned as a Reviewer.

Luca will be implementing US-GTD-8 from docs/user-stories.md. Please review his work as it progresses and send feedback to luca-palmieri from your area of expertise. Key areas to watch:
- Kent: TDD discipline, test-first approach
- Scott: Domain types for routes and navigation state
- Carson: HTMX patterns, hx-boost, route structure
- Lea: CSS for header nav, flexbox wrap, design tokens
- Steve Schoger: Visual hierarchy, spacing, nav design
- Steve Krug: Usability of navigation, trunk test
- Heydon: Nav landmark, aria-current, keyboard nav, skip link
- Marty: Match against US-GTD-8 acceptance criteria
</teammate-message>

content.text: Good -- implementation is starting. Luca is driving the navigation + dashboard skeleton (US-GTD-8). This is the task where the CSS architecture decisions I've been discussing become real code. Let me send Luca a summary of the settled frontend decisions he needs to implement.

input.content:
Luca, Lea here. Welcome to the Driver seat. Here's a summary of the settled CSS and frontend decisions for the navigation + dashboard implementation:

## Header Nav Architecture (Team Consensus)

**Layout**: Single `<nav>` landmark in the header with all GTD view links. No sidebar. Flexbox wrapping on mobile.

**Link order** (Heydon's recommendation, matches GTD workflow priority):
1. Inbox (with count)
2. Next Actions
3. Projects
4. Waiting For
5. Someday/Maybe
6. Review
7. Sign out (always last -- `<form method="post">` with `<button>`, NOT an `<a>` tag)

**Active state**: Use `aria-current="page"` attribute on the current page's nav link. CSS styles off this attribute:
```css
.gtd-header__nav-item[aria-current="page"] {
  font-weight: var(--font-semibold);
  color: var(--color-fg);
  border-block-end-color: var(--color-primary);
}
```

Use a transparent border on all nav items to prevent layout shift:
```css
.gtd-header__nav-item {
  border-block-end: 3px solid transparent;
}
```

**Visual hierarchy** (Steve Schoger's two-tier design):
- Tier 1 (daily drivers): Inbox, Next Actions, Projects -- `--font-medium`
- Tier 2 (less frequent): Waiting For, Someday/Maybe, Review -- `--font-normal`, slightly more muted

**Inbox count**: Visible inline text "Inbox (5)" -- NOT aria-label, NOT a badge. Use a `<span>` wrapper for styling the count differently. When count is zero, just "Inbox" with no number.

**Sign out**: Must remain `<form method="post" action="/logout">` with a `<button>` styled as text link (`.nav-logout__button` pattern we already have). It's a state mutation, not navigation.

## HTMX Pattern

- `hx-boost="true"` on `<body>` in `base.html` -- enhances ALL links and forms automatically
- Nav links are plain `<a href="/inbox">` etc. -- no per-element HTMX attributes needed
- Boost handles `pushState`, history, and body swap transparently
- Server always renders full pages -- no fragment-vs-full-page branching for navigation routes

## Template Hierarchy

- `base.html` stays as the root template with `hx-boost="true"` on `<body>`
- Auth pages (login, register, forgot-password) use their existing `{% block nav %}` content
- GTD pages put the full nav in `{% block nav %}`
- Consider whether a `base_gtd.html` intermediate template is worth it to avoid repeating the nav in every GTD template. If you do, it extends `base.html` and provides the nav block.

## CSS Component Names

- `.gtd-header` -- the authenticated app header
- `.gtd-header__brand` -- app name link
- `.gtd-header__nav` -- the GTD view links container (flexbox, wrapping)
- `.gtd-header__nav-item` -- individual nav link
- `.gtd-header__nav-count` -- inline count span "(5)"
- `.gtd-header__actions` -- sign out area, pushed right with `margin-inline-start: auto`

## Dashboard (US-GTD-8)

The dashboard shows summary counts:
- Inbox count (items needing processing)
- Stalled projects count (projects without next actions)
- Total active next actions count
- Waiting For count

This is a grid of stat cards. Suggested CSS: `.gtd-dashboard__stats` as a CSS Grid container, `.gtd-dashboard__stat` for each card. Use existing tokens for spacing, borders, typography.

## Content Width

Bump the content area to `max-inline-size: 44rem` for GTD pages (wider than the current 40rem to give lists more room). Auth pages stay at 40rem.

Let me know if you need any CSS clarification as you build. I'll be reviewing your work from the frontend perspective.
input.summary: Complete CSS/frontend spec for nav + dashboard implementation

content.text: Comprehensive CSS/frontend spec sent to Luca. He now has everything he needs to implement the nav and dashboard from a frontend perspective -- component names, active state pattern, HTMX approach, visual hierarchy, layout details. Ready to review his work as it progresses.
